figures.caption: Table 5: SCS scores across architectures and labeling pipelines. Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean (in %) across the evaluated neurons and the three label augmentation regimes. The number of neurons using augmented labels (when applicable) included in parenthesis. Best-performing pipelines are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 4: Examples of neurons, their assigned labels by different labeling pipelines, and the corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline label set. Neurons #17170 (SAE-TopK) and #1560 (ResNet50) illustrate CSP’s ability to disentangle co-occurring features. Neuron #28879 (SAE-TopK) shows an over-projection failure case, which CSP with γ = 0.5 mitigates.
Table 9: SCS scores for CSP under different γ values on the ISIC use case using DermLIP as the simulator. Values are reported as mean ± (in %) standard error of the mean. Best variant is in bold.
Fig. 9: Examples of labels predicted by different labeling pipelines and their corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline labels dataset.

Fig. 1: Contrastive neuron explanations improve both label generation and assignment. (a) We form positive (high-activation) and contrastive (semantically similar, low-activation) image sets for a neuron that encodes “carton”. (b) Providing both sets to a VLM yields more specific candidate labels (e.g., “cat in carton”) and more precise ones (e.g., “carton”) than positives alone. (c) Our Contrastive Semantic Projection (CSP) extends CLIP-style scoring by contrasting positive sample embeddings with negative ones, producing more precise and faithful label assignments.

Table 7: DMA scores for CSP using baseline labels only, under different γ values, for ResNet50 and SAE-TopK. Values are reported as mean ± (in %) standard error of the mean. Best variant per architecture is in bold.

Table 8: DMA, AUC, and SCS scores without label augmentation, comparing vanilla CSP, CSP with γ = 0.5, a contrastive score-diff baseline (see Eq. (18)), and a contrastive embedding-diff baseline (see Eq. (19)), for ResNet50 and SAETopK. Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean. Best variant per metric and architecture is shown in bold.

Fig. 3: Improvements from contrastive prompts. (a) Faithfulness gains measured via SCS across multiple InternVL model sizes. Contrastive prompts (orange) consistently increase SCS relative to positive-only prompts (blue). (b) Discriminativeness improvements measured via cosine-similarity label alignment on positive and contrastive data examples. Contrastive prompts improve both label alignment to positive and reduce alignment to contrastive examples. (c) Qualitative examples illustrating improved concept isolation. For two representative neurons, positive-only prompts yield labels tied to broad or co-occurring attributes (e.g., human activity), while contrastive prompts highlight more specific concepts (e.g., women only). We provide SCS scores in parenthesis.

Table 2: AUC and SCS faithfulness scores averaged over all architectures, across different labeling pipelines and candidate label sets. Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean (in % for SCS) across the evaluated neurons. Bestperforming pipelines are highlighted in bold.

Fig. 15: Examples of labels predicted by different labeling pipelines and their corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline labels dataset. These examples showcase cases where CSP fails due to over-projection.
Fig. 14: Examples of improving labeling faithfulness (via DMA score) after augmenting the labels with contrastive images.

Fig. 6: Example neurons from the skin-lesion setting. Labeling results (with SCS scores) are shown for each pipeline under positive-only and contrastive augmentation. CSP recovers concepts present in positives but absent from contrastives.

Table 6: DMA scores for labeling pipelines using baseline labels only, under different values k of positive and contrastive samples. Only CSP makes use of the contrastive samples. LE is k-agnostic as it relies on the entire activation range and is therefore reported as a separate row. Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean (in %). For each k value, the best-performing pipeline is highlighted in bold.

Table 1: DMA faithfulness scores across architectures, labeling pipelines and candidate label sets (dataset annotations, and VLM-based labels without and with contrastive examples). Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean (in %) across the evaluated neurons and the three label augmentation regimes. The number of neurons using augmented labels (when applicable) included in parenthesis. Best-performing pipelines are highlighted in bold.
Fig. 12: Examples of improving labeling faithfulness (via DMA score) after augmenting the labels with contrastive images.
Fig. 13: Examples of improving labeling faithfulness (via DMA score) after augmenting the labels with contrastive images.
Fig. 8: Examples of labels predicted by different labeling pipelines and their corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline labels dataset.
Fig. 7: Examples of labels predicted by different labeling pipelines and their corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline labels dataset.

Table 4: AUC scores across architectures and labeling pipelines. Values are reported as mean ± standard error of the mean across the evaluated neurons and the three label augmentation regimes. The number of neurons using augmented labels (when applicable) included in parenthesis. Best-performing pipelines are highlighted in bold.
Fig. 11: Examples of improving labeling faithfulness (via DMA score) after augmenting the labels with contrastive images.
Fig. 10: Examples of labels predicted by different labeling pipelines and their corresponding DMA scores, using only the baseline labels dataset.

Fig. 2: Contrastive neuron labeling with CSP. (a) For each neuron, we construct a positive image set with high activation and a contrastive set that is semantically similar but weakly activating. (b) Providing both sets to a vision–language model yields more specific candidate labels by explicitly contrasting the two sets. (c) Contrastive Semantic Projection (CSP) embeds candidate labels and scores them using positive minus contrastive similarity.

Fig. 5: Examples of neurons, their assigned labels by different labeling pipelines, and the corresponding DMA scores, under the positive-only and contrastive labeling strategies. Only CSP selects the best-performing label introduced by contrastive augmentation for both neurons shown.
